name: Dutch A1 Vocabulary
description: Beginner Dutch. Basic phrases, numbers, days of the week. Enough for Amsterdam without a phrasebook.

facts.explanation: aardig — friendly, nice. Also: nice, considerable, substantial
alleen — alone, by oneself. Also: lonely
altijd — always.
ander — other. Also: different, second
auto — car, automobile.
bang — scared, frightened. Also: fearful, anxious
bank — bench. Also: couch, sofa, place where seashells are found, shallow part of the sea near the coast
beet — bite.
begrijpen — to understand (concepts, ideas), to comprehend, to grasp. Also: to encompass, include
bellen — to ring, like using a bell. Also: to call, by bell (originally) or (now mostly) telephone; to dial
betalen — to pay. Also: to pay for, suffer the consequences of
blaffen — to bark, like a canine and certain others species. Also: to shout rudely etc., to grumble, notably said of a hungry stomach, to utter pointless sounds, e.g. protest or argue in vain
blij — happy; momentarily. For generally in life, see gelukkig.. Also: glad
boek — book. Also: omasum
boot — boat.
brief — letter (written message).
broer — brother (male sibling). Also: bro

bus — a container, a box, a tin. Also: a bushing, one of a variety of early modern firearms, such as flintlock and matchlock guns, a voluntary sick fund, especially before the introduction of universal health care in the Netherlands in the 1940s
buschauffeur — bus driver.
dag — day (period of 24 hours). Also: daytime (time between sunrise and sunset), a meeting or assembly with legal or political power, originally convened on a specific day; a diet
dan — then, at that time (in the future). Also: then, after that, then, in that case
danken — to thank.
dansen — to dance.
dansfeest — party involving dance.
denken — to think.
ding — matter, thing. Also: thing (popular assembly or judicial council in early Germanic society)

doen — to do. Also: to put, to cause to, to make; forms causative verbs, to give, serve, bring, to do, to have sex with someone, to touch a nerve; to strike a chord in, to be different; to make a difference, to behave or act in some way, Used as the finite verb after the fronting of an infinitive., Added to sentences without a specific meaning, when there is no other auxiliary.
drinken — to drink, consume a liquid. Also: to be an alcoholic
en — not (often with another negator, see usage notes). Also: only, merely, no other than, none other than (together with maar)
erg — serious, considerable, severe. Also: awful, terrible
eten — food. Also: dinner
familie — relatives (extended kin). Also: family (close kin), family
fiets — bicycle. Also: motorbike
fijn — nice, pleasant. Also: fine, thin, delicate, meticulous
fles — bottle.

gaan — to go, to move from one place to another. Also: to leave or depart, to move away, to lead (in a direction), to proceed (well or poorly), Forms the future tense of a verb, similarly to English going to future tense, to start to, begin to, to be going to, to be about, to be the point of, to be about, to be in charge of, to experience, to take, to feel some way about something
geld — not pregnant. Also: male
geven — to give. Also: to have a negative effect, to care about

goed — good (not bad). Also: correct, right (factually or morally), all right, fine, considerable (in amount or size), at least as much as, given, certain (unknown, undetermined or unspecified) (used in prepositional phrases with op)
graag — willingly, gladly (meaning that the subject enjoys doing the action of the verb).
groot — big, large, great. Also: great, important, eminent, tall, adult
haar — hair (collection of hairs). Also: hair (mammalian keratin filament), trichome (hair-like growth on a plant), a bit, minute quantity

hebben — to have, to possess, own, hold. Also: to be afflicted with a disease, Used to form the perfect tense of the active voice of most verbs, together with a past participle., must, to be obliged to, to be to
heel — complete, full, whole. Also: intact, unbroken, undamaged, untarnished, big, significant
helpen — to help. Also: to provide, to supply, to get hooked, to cause (someone) to be very keen (on), addicted or depended

heten — to be named; to have as name; to be called. Also: to be (a translation or synonym of); to be called, to name, to call (to provide with a name, to define a name as), to be claimed; to be asserted, to mean; to have as meaning
hoe — how.
hond — dog (Canis lupus familiaris). Also: A derogatory term for a human; a reprehensible person
horen — to hear. Also: to belong, to be supposed to, to be meant to, to be appropriate, polite or agreeing with social norms (lit. to be heard of)
houden — to keep, preserve. Also: to keep, to care for, to hold, to be engaged in, to hold, to have, to organise (an event), to love; to like, to take for, to consider, to see as

huis — a house, home; residence. Also: an adjoining building with a separate function, a genealogical house, such as a dynasty, a house or chamber in a legislative assembly, a housing structure, casing
ja — yes.

jaar — year (the time it takes the Earth to circle the Sun; its equivalent on other planets). Also: year (the individuals associated with a year, notably the students in or graduates from an annual class at school)

kamer — a room, physical chamber in a building. Also: a bedroom, a living room, a special, more luxurious living room (formerly) reserved for use on Sundays and holidays, found in old houses that were inhabited by relatively affluent people, a non-campus residence for students, a board, judicial court or other institution with a specific competence, a chamber or division of a legisative assembly such as a parliament, a separated section in a structured object, e.g. chamber of a gun
keer — time (occasion, instance). Also: reversion, reversal, turn

kennen — to know (a person, a thing), be acquainted with, to have knowledge of the properties of a particular person, object or concept, through personal experience, teaching, practice, or habit; to be familiar with. Also: to know, experience, alternative form of kunnen
keuken — kitchen. Also: cuisine
kijken — to look. Also: to watch (e.g. a film), to observe
kind — child, kid, non-adult human. Also: first-degree descendant, still a minor or irrespective of age, product of influence, breeding etc.
kleed — cloth. Also: long cloth garment, notably:, formal robe, female dress, rug, mat, plumage
klein — small, little. Also: almost
koffie — coffee (beverage). Also: coffee (serving of the above beverage)
komen — to come. Also: to happen, to arise, to come to be, to be caused, to become, synonym of klaarkomen (“to orgasm, come”)
kopen — to buy, purchase.

krijgen — to get, to receive, to come into possession of (anything ranging from concrete to abstract inputs such as news, gift, punishment, et cetera). Also: to get, to be presented with, to get, to (manage to) cause to become, Used to form a passive sentence with a ditransitive verb, with the original indirect object becoming the subject of krijgen., to catch (a disease), to become ill with

kunnen — can, to be able to. Also: may, can, to be allowed to, to be possible, can go, to be able to go, can get, to be able to do, to be capable of, to be available (for any type of meeting or appointment)
laat — late (not early). Also: tardy
lachen — to laugh.
lang — long. Also: tall, long (time), lengthy, a long time
leerling — a learner, someone who learns. Also: someone formally enrolled for some education: a pupil, student, apprentice, disciple

lekker — having a nice taste, tasty, delectable. Also: good, nice, pleasant, satisfying in a more generic sense, hot, sexy, physically attractive, Healthy, well, in good health (not afflicted by illness), sound of mind, sane
les — course, lesson.
leuk — nice, pleasant, enjoyable. Also: attractive, calm, level-headed, lukewarm
lezen — to read. Also: to gather (esp. fruits)
liggen — to lie; to be lying down. Also: to be, to be placed or located, Forms a continuous aspect., to be one's thing; to suit

lopen — to run, go fast on one's own legs. Also: to run, cover distance, follow a track etc., to walk, go somewhere regardless of speed, to walk in general, to stretch, to run (to extend in space or through a range) (often of relatively elongated objects or constructs), to be current, activated, in progress, to be doing or functioning (well or poorly), to progress, to lose liquid: drip, gush, leak, Forms a continuous aspect.
maken — to make, create, prepare. Also: to fix, to repair, to mend, to make, cause to become
man — man, human male, either adult or age-irrespective. Also: husband, male spouse
meisje — girl (especially of a premature age). Also: girlfriend
mens — human, any member of the species Homo sapiens. Also: person
met — mince (sometimes specifically uncooked).
mevrouw — madam, miss.

mijn — mine (place where ore is taken out of the ground). Also: mine (device meant to explode when stepped upon or touched), mine (tunnel used for sapping enemy defence works or lines), shaft dug by an insect larva in plant organs, such as leaves
misschien — perhaps, maybe.
moeder — mother, female parent. Also: womb
moeten — to be necessary, to be required. Also: to have to, must, should, be meant to, be supposed to, to have to go, to need to go, must go, to need to go to the toilet
mogen — to be allowed. Also: may, can, be allowed to, may go, can go, to be allowed to go, may have, can have, to like (of food or of a person)
mooi — beautiful, pretty, handsome. Also: nice, good
morgen — tomorrow.
muziek — music (sound; art).
naam — name. Also: reputation
niet — not, no, don't: used to express negation..
nieuw — new.
nodig — necessary, needed. Also: inevitable

nog — still, as before. Also: yet, more, in addition, to indicate the time is soon, soon after another event or within the same timespan → as early as, already, to indicate the time is recent or just before another event → as recently as, as late as, just
nooit — never.
normaal — normal, usual, in accordance with what is common. Also: normal, perpendicular
nu — now, at the present moment. Also: now, this time (indicating a certain amount of impatience)
ons — metric ounce (100 grams).
oog — eye. Also: pip (of dice), eye of a needle, eye (of a peacock's tail), eye (of a storm), sight, vision, gaze
ook — also, too, moreover. Also: either, -ever, particle for emphasis
oud — old (having existed for a relatively long period of time). Also: old (of an earlier time), stale
ouder — parent, father or mother; by extension a substitute caregiver.
pakken — to grab, to take. Also: to get, to fetch, to pack, to compact, to wrap up
praten — to talk.
probleem — problem.
rennen — to run, to move fast.
rijden — to ride. Also: to drive
rood — red. Also: left, socialist, labor
samen — together.

school — a school, educational institution that provides education, whether combined with research or not. Also: a thematic educational institute within a larger one, such as in a university for a single research field, any organisation providing instruction, a movement or stylistic trend
schreeuwen — to yell; to bellow; to shout; to talk loudly. Also: to scream; to shriek, of a colour or design: to be extremely conspicuous
schrijven — to write. Also: to write (data)
slaapkamer — bedroom.
slapen — to sleep.
snel — fast, quick, rapid.
soms — sometimes. Also: maybe, perhaps
spreken — to speak.

staan — to stand, to be upright. Also: to be, to be placed or located, Forms a continuous aspect. Although it carries an implication of standing, this is vague and is not strictly required or emphasized., to be present, have a stand or location, to say, to be written, to be said (to be indicated in written form), to suit, fit, befit (of clothing), Indicates a prospective outcome, or something that will happen in the near future
stok — stick, natural (wood) or artificial. Also: cane, deck, stock (set of playing cards), stock, supply
strand — beach, strand.
terug — backwards, back (in the opposite direction). Also: back, to a previous place or condition, back, in return, ago, back (in time), again, back again
thuis — at home (at one's dwelling or in one's home area).
trein — train (railway vehicle). Also: train, convoy, retinue, procession
tuin — a garden, yard. Also: an enclosure, fence or hedge
vaak — often.
vader — father, male parent. Also: forefather
vakantie — a holiday, vacation. Also: an easy, careless time
veel — much. Also: often, frequently
verdrietig — sad (chiefly in relation to the emotions of human and animals).
vertellen — to tell. Also: to miscount
vertrekken — to depart, to leave.
vinden — to find (after searching). Also: to find, to come across, to find, to think, to feel, to be of opinion
vliegveld — airfield. Also: airport
volgen — to follow (go after). Also: to follow (understand, pay attention to), to take, to follow
vragen — to ask (a question). Also: to demand, exact, to require, need
vriend — friend. Also: boyfriend (romantic partner)
vriendin — female friend. Also: girlfriend (unmarried female romantic partner)
vroeg — early.
vrouw — woman. Also: wife
week — soft, tender, fragile. Also: weak, gentle, weakhearted
weer — defense. Also: resistance, seisin

wel — the opposite of not (used to add positive emphasis to the verb, much like the auxiliaries do or does in affirmative sentences in English). Also: no less than, as much as, as many as (expressing amazement), fairly, well
werk — work, labor. Also: profession, job, employment, line of work, workplace, product, creation; production, output, result of work, tow, oakum
werken — to work, labour. Also: to function, work, be in working order, to warp, settle, to contact
weten — to know (knowledge), to be aware of something. Also: to remember, to be able to, to manage to (literally, "to know how to/to know of a way to")
willen — to want, desire. Also: to want to, will, be willing to, to want to go somewhere (cf. English want in), to wish, would like, would (as in dated English I would that)
winkel — shop, store (establishment that sells goods to the public). Also: a shop, service point, a corner, as in winkelhaak
wonen — to live (in a certain place).
worden — to become, to get, to grow, to turn. Also: Used to form the imperfect tense of the passive voice, together with a past participle.
zacht — soft. Also: gentle, mild (of weather), with low calcium content (of water)
zeggen — to say, tell.
zien — to see.
zij — side (the side of one's body); flank.

zijn — to be, to exist. Also: Used to connect a noun to an adjective that describes it., Used to form the perfect tense of the active voice of some verbs, together with a past participle., Used to form the perfect tense of the active voice of some verbs, together with a past participle. Note: The perfect tense of most other verbs is formed using hebben., Note: The perfect tense of most other verbs is formed using hebben., Used to form the perfect tense of the passive voice, together with a past participle., Used to form the perfect tense of the passive voice, together with a past participle. Note: The imperfect tense passive is formed using worden., Note: The imperfect tense passive is formed using worden., Used to form the continuous forms of various tenses, together with aan het or, in archaic style, with a present participle., to go, to go on a trip and return, Used to indicate weather, temperature or some other general condition., to be, to equal, to total, to amount to; used to indicate that the values on either side of an equation are the same.

zitten — to sit. Also: to be, to be placed or located, Forms a continuous aspect. Although it carries an implication of sitting, this is vague and is not strictly required or emphasized., to have a meeting, to consult, to sit down, to be in prison
zo — so, thus, like that/this. Also: so, that, to such an extent, as .. as, right away, in a second/minute, very soon
zoeken — to search. Also: to look for, to seek
zomer — summer.

zullen — used to form the future tense of a verb, together with an infinitive; will, going to. Also: used to form the conditional mood of a verb, together with an infinitive; would, used to indicate intent or disposition to perform an action in the future: will, shall, be going to, may, might
zus — sister.

questionTemplates.questionFormat: Which word is closest in meaning to '{targetLanguageWord}'?
